title: Treating scaly leg mite
subtitle: Smothering the mite that burrows under leg scales in laying hens.
excerpt: Scaly leg mite makes the leg scales lift, crust, and thicken until walking becomes painful. The treatment is straightforward: suffocate the mite by coating the legs thoroughly with petroleum jelly or similar barrier product, repeated weekly for four to six weeks.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code of Practice for Laying Hens (2018). BHWT Henkeeper health reference. Smallholder Magazine, external parasite features.

content.text: Scaly leg mite progresses slowly. Early infestation shows as slightly raised leg scales on otherwise healthy birds. By the time the legs are heavily crusted and the scales are flaking off in chunks, the bird has had the mite for months or years. Check legs routinely when you catch birds for other reasons; early treatment is quick and the outcome is better.
and examine the legs from the toes to the feathered hock. Normal leg scales sit flat and smooth with tight edges.
causes the scales to lift at the edges and a white powdery or yellowish crust to form underneath. In advanced cases the entire lower leg becomes encrusted and the scales deform permanently.

The standard UK treatment is petroleum jelly (Vaseline), applied thickly to the legs and feet from toe to hock. Work it into the gaps between the scales and under the lifted edges. The mite breathes through the scale surface; the barrier suffocates it. Repeat once a week for four to six weeks. After each application the legs should soften and the scales begin to lie flat again.

A licenced spray such as ivermectin pour-on (from the vet) is more reliable for bad infestations, where the mite has dug in deep under the scales. This is a prescription product. The vet will tell you how to use it and how long to wait before eating the eggs.

The mite is transmitted by direct contact between birds. Check all other birds in the flock when you find one infected bird, and treat any showing lifted scales at the same time. Treat perches by spraying or wiping with a diluted poultry-licenced disinfectant; the mite can survive off the bird for several weeks in crevices.
